prompt:
Telemetry-ingest ticket. Our batch scorer walks a slice of readings, maps each fixed-size chunk to a partial result on a worker thread, and merges the partials. The prototype wrapped everything in Arc<Vec<T>> and cloned readings into each worker, which (a) forced T: Clone onto callers that don't have it and (b) made results own copies when callers want to hand back references INTO the input (e.g. "the max element"). Rust has had std::thread::scope since 1.63 — workers can borrow the slice directly. Rewrite it that way.

This crate is rs-scopedproc (lib name rs_scopedproc, Cargo.toml already in place — leave it and .gitignore alone). Stdlib only, no unsafe, no Arc anywhere in this feature. Everything in src/. The acceptance contract is tests/scopedproc.rs, byte for byte as checked in: `cargo test --offline` fully green, test file untouched.

Public API, exactly one function plus its error enum:

- `pub enum ChunkError { ZeroChunkSize, ZeroWorkers }` — derive Debug, PartialEq, Eq.
- `pub fn process_chunks<'a, T, R, M, G>(items: &'a [T], chunk_size: usize, workers: usize, map_fn: M, merge_fn: G) -> Result<Option<R>, ChunkError>` where `T: Sync`, `R: Send`, `M: Fn(usize, &'a [T]) -> R + Sync`, `G: FnMut(R, R) -> R`. Note the lifetimes: map results may borrow from `items` (the contract asserts pointer identity on a returned `&u32`), so the map closure takes `&'a [T]`, not a higher-ranked borrow.

Semantics, exactly as the tests pin them:

- Validate first, in this order: chunk_size == 0 is ZeroChunkSize, then workers == 0 is ZeroWorkers — BOTH checked before the empty-input shortcut, so garbage arguments never pass just because the slice was empty.
- Empty input: Ok(None), and map_fn must never run.
- Chunking: contiguous, in order, chunk i covers items[i*chunk_size ..], last chunk ragged. map_fn receives (chunk_index, sub-slice).
- Assignment is round-robin over min(workers, number_of_chunks) scoped worker threads: worker w owns chunks w, w+n, w+2n, ... and processes its own chunks in ascending index order. Spawn only that many threads — 16 requested workers over 3 chunks means exactly 3 worker threads.
- Merge happens on the calling thread after the workers are done, as a LEFT FOLD in chunk-index order: (((r0 merge r1) merge r2) merge r3) — regardless of which worker finished first. The contract includes a channel-gated test where chunk 0 provably finishes after chunk 1; completion order must not leak into the merge.
- Workers borrow the input; nothing is cloned. T carries no Clone/Default bounds and one test uses a deliberately non-Clone struct.

Don't add public API beyond these two items, don't sleep or poll anywhere, and don't fall back to processing on the caller thread when workers >= 1 (the gate test deadlocks any sequential implementation and its 10-second backstop will name the bug). Same input must produce byte-identical output on every run — the fold order guarantees that for free if you implement it as specified.
